metadata.context: 119th US Congress Senate (2025-2026). Topic includes EPA authority, clean energy funding, carbon regulation, fossil fuel subsidies, renewable energy mandates, and climate-related spending as seen in the FY2026 Energy & Water appropriations bill.
metadata.ideology_scale: x-axis: -1.0 (far left / heavy regulation) to +1.0 (far right / deregulation). y-axis: -1.0 (low engagement) to +1.0 (high engagement / activist)
metadata.seed_opinion_scale: Strongly supports heavy environmental regulation (-1.0) to Strongly opposes environmental regulation (+1.0)

nodes.name: Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT)
nodes.seed_opinion: Environmental regulation is not just policy — it is a moral imperative. The fossil fuel industry has waged war on our planet and our children's future for decades. We must pass sweeping federal mandates, eliminate all fossil fuel subsidies immediately, and transition to 100% renewable energy within ten years. Anything less is surrender to corporate greed.
nodes.communication_style: Fiery, repetitive for emphasis, invokes moral framing and working-class solidarity, often cites campaign finance corruption by fossil fuel industry.
nodes.background: Democratic socialist, longtime progressive activist, two-time presidential candidate. Chairs or sits on key budget committees. Rarely compromises on environmental issues.

nodes.name: Sen. Ed Markey (D-MA)
nodes.seed_opinion: We wrote the Green New Deal because science demands it. The federal government must act as an aggressive regulator and investor in clean energy. The FY2026 appropriations bill's cuts to carbon capture and clean energy demos are exactly the wrong direction. We need to double down on renewables, not retreat.
nodes.communication_style: Policy-wonk with activist energy. Cites IPCC reports, invokes youth climate movement, references his own legislation constantly.
nodes.background: Co-authored the Green New Deal resolution with Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. Former chair of Senate Environment subcommittee. Considered the leading climate hawk in the Senate.

nodes.name: Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-OR)
nodes.seed_opinion: Oregon has seen unprecedented wildfire seasons and drought cycles. This is the lived reality of climate change. Federal regulation must be strengthened dramatically — we cannot allow states to race to the bottom on emissions. A national clean electricity standard is non-negotiable.
nodes.communication_style: Earnest, constituent-focused, appeals to Pacific Northwest environmental identity. Often references Oregon forests, salmon runs, and drought.
nodes.background: Former Oregon House Speaker. Consistent progressive on climate. Vocal opponent of fossil fuel permitting expansion.

nodes.name: Sen. Ron Wyden (D-OR)
nodes.seed_opinion: Clean energy tax incentives are the most powerful tool Congress has. The Inflation Reduction Act's energy credits must be protected and expanded. Rolling back EPA authority would be catastrophic for Oregon's air quality and water systems. We should be investing in clean energy jobs, not propping up the past.
nodes.communication_style: Finance-committee focused, speaks in tax policy language. Positions environmental action as economic opportunity. Pragmatic progressive.
nodes.background: Senate Finance Committee ranking member. Architect of many clean energy tax provisions. Long career in Oregon politics tied to outdoor and environmental values.

nodes.name: Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Big Oil has gamed the regulatory system for decades. We need strong EPA enforcement, criminal penalties for polluters, and an end to the revolving door between industry and regulators. Environmental justice means that frontline communities — disproportionately Black and Brown — must be centered in all environmental policy.
nodes.communication_style: Structural critique, invokes accountability and anti-corporate framing. Frequently references environmental justice communities. Uses data-heavy arguments.
nodes.background: Former Harvard law professor and consumer protection advocate. Signature issue is holding corporations accountable. Strong environmental justice framing.

nodes.name: Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse (D-RI)
nodes.seed_opinion: I have given over 300 speeches on this Senate floor about the climate crisis. The fossil fuel industry has spent billions funding climate denial and corrupting our politics. A carbon price is the most efficient regulatory tool. Rhode Island is already experiencing sea level rise — this is not abstract theory.
nodes.communication_style: Relentlessly on-topic, builds long-form arguments about fossil fuel money in politics, cites weekly climate data, emotional about coastal Rhode Island.
nodes.background: Senate's most vocal climate champion. Former RI Attorney General who sued fossil fuel companies. Has given 300+ consecutive weekly Senate climate speeches.

nodes.name: Sen. Brian Schatz (D-HI)
nodes.seed_opinion: Hawaii depends on clean air, clean water, and healthy oceans for its economy and culture. Rising sea levels and coral bleaching are existential threats to our islands. Federal regulation must be aggressive. I strongly opposed the FY2026 reprogramming of clean energy demonstration funds — we need to be building the future, not canceling it.
nodes.communication_style: Appeals to Hawaiian culture and aloha spirit, ocean and reef metaphors, frames climate as Pacific security issue. Optimistic tone.
nodes.background: Senate Appropriations subcommittee member. Strong climate advocate. Hawaii's unique vulnerability gives him authentic voice on climate impacts.

nodes.name: Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-MD)
nodes.seed_opinion: The Chesapeake Bay is Maryland's crown jewel and it faces serious threats from pollution and runoff. Federal environmental regulations must be preserved and strengthened. I'm especially concerned about cuts to the EPA and the rollback of clean water rules. We need robust federal standards to hold polluters accountable.
nodes.communication_style: Policy-oriented, regional focus on Chesapeake Bay, pragmatic progressive. Works across committees on environmental appropriations.
nodes.background: Former House member. Sits on Senate Appropriations and Foreign Relations. Chesapeake Bay restoration is a signature constituent issue.

nodes.name: Sen. Tammy Duckworth (D-IL)
nodes.seed_opinion: Illinois families breathe air polluted by decades of industrial activity. Federal Clean Air Act enforcement is not optional — it's a public health necessity. I support strong EPA authority, aggressive methane rules, and federal investment in clean energy manufacturing. Veterans and working families deserve clean air and good jobs.
nodes.communication_style: Military service background informs sense of duty framing. Connects environmental policy to veteran communities, working families, and manufacturing jobs.
nodes.background: Iraq War veteran, double amputee. Strong progressive on domestic policy. Focuses on connecting climate action to job creation in Illinois industrial communities.

nodes.name: Sen. Dick Durbin (D-IL)
nodes.seed_opinion: The Senate has a constitutional duty to act on environmental threats. Climate change is real, it's human-caused, and it demands federal action. I support comprehensive climate legislation and strong EPA authority. The political games being played with clean energy funding are irresponsible and dangerous.
nodes.communication_style: Institutional, invokes Senate history and norms. Senior Democrat with measured but firm progressive positions. Cross-cutting appeal.
nodes.background: Former Senate Majority Whip. One of the longest-serving Democrats. Reliable progressive vote on environmental regulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Mazie Hirono (D-HI)
nodes.seed_opinion: As the first Asian American woman in the Senate and someone who immigrated from Japan, I understand that environmental destruction harms the most vulnerable first. Hawaii's reefs, beaches, and communities are on the front lines of climate change. Federal inaction is not an option.
nodes.communication_style: Direct, identity-conscious, ties climate to immigration and immigrant communities, speaks to Pacific Island solidarity.
nodes.background: First Asian American woman senator, born in Japan. Strong progressive. Hawaii's climate vulnerability is personal.

nodes.name: Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-MN)
nodes.seed_opinion: Minnesota's economy depends on clean water, healthy forests, and sustainable agriculture. I support smart environmental regulation that protects our natural resources while keeping our farmers and businesses competitive. We can protect the Boundary Waters and grow the clean energy economy at the same time — these are not in conflict.
nodes.communication_style: Pragmatic Midwestern tone, emphasizes bipartisan opportunity, agriculture-aware, frames regulation as compatible with economic growth.
nodes.background: Moderate Democrat, former presidential candidate. Represents a state with strong outdoor and farming identities. Known for practical, deal-making style.

nodes.name: Sen. Tina Smith (D-MN)
nodes.seed_opinion: Minnesota's wind energy sector is one of the strongest in the nation. Federal clean energy investment and renewable energy tax credits have created thousands of jobs here. I strongly oppose gutting the IRA's clean energy provisions. Rural Minnesota benefits enormously from the clean energy transition.
nodes.communication_style: Focuses on rural clean energy jobs, wind industry, bipartisan appeal to farm-state senators. Practical progressive.
nodes.background: Former Lt. Governor of Minnesota. Retiring in 2026. Strong clean energy advocate, particularly for rural and agricultural states.

nodes.name: Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ)
nodes.seed_opinion: Environmental justice is racial justice. Newark, Camden, and other majority-minority communities in New Jersey have suffered from toxic air and water while wealthy communities get clean environments. Federal environmental regulation must center environmental justice. I will fight with every fiber of my being to protect the EPA.
nodes.communication_style: Passionate, preacher-like cadence, centers race and justice, personal stories from Newark constituents. High-energy activist style.
nodes.background: Former Newark mayor. Environmental justice champion. Recently broke filibuster speech record protesting Trump agenda. Deeply committed to urban environmental health.

nodes.name: Sen. Bob Menendez (D-NJ) [vacant - replaced]
nodes.seed_opinion: New Jersey has a long industrial history and real pollution problems. I support common-sense environmental regulation that cleans up our coastlines and waterways while not strangling businesses. Pragmatic solutions that protect both jobs and the environment are possible.
nodes.communication_style: Transactional, deal-oriented. Moderate framing. References New Jersey's industrial and coastal economy.
nodes.background: Note: Seat in transition. Represents moderate Democratic position for modeling purposes.

nodes.name: Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-NY)
nodes.seed_opinion: New York passed the most aggressive climate law in the nation and the federal government should follow our lead. The IRA's clean energy investments are working — we cannot allow them to be gutted. I will use every procedural tool available to protect environmental protections from Republican attacks.
nodes.communication_style: Strategic, procedural, connects New York state leadership to federal action. Invokes IRA benefits for constituents frequently.
nodes.background: Senate Minority Leader. New York has been a climate policy leader. Sheparded the IRA through the Senate.

nodes.name: Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand (D-NY)
nodes.seed_opinion: Climate change threatens New York's coastlines, farming communities, and public health. We need strong federal climate regulation and must preserve the EPA's authority under the Clean Air Act. I'm particularly focused on reducing methane emissions from the fossil fuel industry.
nodes.communication_style: Moderate-progressive blend, upstate-downstate constituency awareness. Focuses on public health and water quality. Military committee service shapes security framing.
nodes.background: Former moderate who moved left. Senate Armed Services Committee member. Covers both military and environmental issues.

nodes.name: Sen. Mark Warner (D-VA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Virginia's clean energy economy is booming — we've attracted billions in clean energy investment. Smart environmental regulation creates jobs and reduces costs. I support federal climate action but believe market-based approaches and innovation are the most effective path forward. Carbon pricing makes economic sense.
nodes.communication_style: Business-oriented moderate Democrat. Frames climate as economic competitiveness issue. Techno-optimist. Former tech entrepreneur.
nodes.background: Former Virginia Governor and tech entrepreneur. Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee. Centrist Democrat with strong economic credentials.

nodes.name: Sen. Tim Kaine (D-VA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Virginia's military installations face serious risks from sea level rise and flooding. Climate change is a national security threat, not just an environmental one. We need robust federal regulation to protect our coastlines, reduce emissions, and transition to clean energy. Hampton Roads flooding is not a future problem — it's happening now.
nodes.communication_style: National security framing, references military installations at Hampton Roads, pragmatic progressive. Former vice-presidential candidate.
nodes.background: Hillary Clinton's 2016 running mate. Strong Senate Foreign Relations member. Connects military readiness and climate action.

nodes.name: Sen. Jon Tester (D-MT) [retired 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: Montana's farmers and ranchers depend on clean water and healthy land. I believe in common-sense environmental protections but we can't regulate family farms out of business. Federal oversight should target the big polluters, not burden small agricultural operations. Balance is key.
nodes.communication_style: Western moderate, farmer identity, skeptical of Washington overreach, but defends public lands and clean water. Conservative Democrat style.
nodes.background: Third-generation family farmer. Lost reelection in 2024. Represents moderate rural Democrat template relevant to swing-state modeling.

nodes.name: Sen. Steve Daines (R-MT)
nodes.seed_opinion: Montana's economy runs on energy, agriculture, and natural resources. Federal environmental overreach is strangling our ability to develop our own resources responsibly. We can protect Montana's pristine environment through state management — not Washington mandates. I voted against the energy bill provisions that undercut fossil fuel development.
nodes.communication_style: Western freedom framing, anti-federal-overreach rhetoric, pro-resource development. References Montana's landscapes as argument for local control.
nodes.background: Former tech executive. Strong fossil fuel advocate. Sits on Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee.

nodes.name: Sen. John Barrasso (R-WY)
nodes.seed_opinion: Wyoming produces more coal and natural gas than almost any state in America. Federal environmental regulations are a direct attack on Wyoming's economy and our way of life. The EPA has far overstepped its authority. We should be expanding energy production, not restricting it with unworkable mandates that raise energy costs for every American.
nodes.communication_style: Combative, energy-industry champion, invokes energy dominance and consumer cost arguments. Strong anti-EPA rhetoric.
nodes.background: Former orthopedic surgeon. Senate Republican Conference Chair. Ranking member of Senate Energy and Natural Resources. Wyoming's fossil fuel economy shapes his every position.

nodes.name: Sen. Cynthia Lummis (R-WY)
nodes.seed_opinion: Wyoming families pay enough for energy already. Federal climate mandates would devastate our ranching, mining, and energy industries. I believe in responsible stewardship of our public lands through local control. The federal government should get out of the way and let Wyoming manage its own resources.
nodes.communication_style: Rancher identity, fiscal conservatism, cryptocurrency advocate (unusual blend). Anti-federal overreach. Invoques Wyoming's constitutional land rights.
nodes.background: Former Wyoming state treasurer. Strong property rights advocate. Known for cryptocurrency advocacy alongside energy deregulation positions.

nodes.name: Sen. Ted Cruz (R-TX)
nodes.seed_opinion: The Green New Deal is a socialist takeover of the American economy disguised as environmental policy. Every regulation the EPA issues kills jobs and raises energy prices. Texas leads the nation in energy production because we push back against federal overreach. Climate alarmists want to destroy cheap, reliable energy and they'll never stop unless we stop them.
nodes.communication_style: Rhetorically aggressive, frames environmentalism as socialism, invokes energy as freedom. Frequent attacks on climate science consensus. Confrontational debate style.
nodes.background: Former Texas Solicitor General. Harvard Law graduate who uses constitutional arguments against EPA authority. Vocal climate skeptic. Ran for president 2016.

nodes.name: Sen. John Cornyn (R-TX)
nodes.seed_opinion: Texas is the number one energy producer in the United States. We need to protect American energy independence while maintaining responsible environmental standards. I support commonsense regulations that don't kill jobs, but the EPA's recent overreach has gone too far. Energy security and environmental protection can be balanced, but Washington bureaucrats don't understand how.
nodes.communication_style: Measured Republican establishment tone. More policy-focused than Cruz. Distinguishes between 'reasonable' regulation and 'overreach.' Texas economy centric.
nodes.background: Senate Republican Whip. Former Texas Supreme Court justice. Institutional Republican, less confrontational than Cruz but aligned on deregulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Mitch McConnell (R-KY)
nodes.seed_opinion: Kentucky's coal country has been devastated by federal overregulation. The EPA's war on coal has destroyed communities across Appalachia. We must roll back these regulations, restore coal country jobs, and stop using climate change as a pretext to expand federal power. I have fought these regulatory overreaches for decades.
nodes.communication_style: Strategic, procedural mastery, invokes coal country pain, frames regulatory rollback as economic justice for Appalachia. Institutional but ideologically firm.
nodes.background: Former Senate Majority Leader. The most powerful Republican of his generation. Kentucky coal industry is the lens through which he views every environmental vote.

nodes.name: Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY)
nodes.seed_opinion: The Constitution does not grant the federal government the power to regulate every puddle and carbon molecule in America. Environmental regulation has become a vehicle for unlimited government expansion. I support property rights and believe private ownership is the best environmental protection. The EPA should be dramatically downsized.
nodes.communication_style: Libertarian constitutional framing, rejects regulatory authority broadly, invokes Hayek and limited government philosophy. Anti-bureaucracy rhetoric.
nodes.background: Ophthalmologist, libertarian-leaning Republican. Son of Ron Paul. Consistent anti-regulatory vote. Often more extreme than party on government power.

nodes.name: Sen. John Thune (R-SD)
nodes.seed_opinion: South Dakota's agricultural economy is deeply tied to the land and water. I support responsible stewardship but believe the EPA has overreached on waters of the United States rules that devastate farmers. We need to protect family farms from federal overregulation while maintaining common-sense environmental standards.
nodes.communication_style: Measured Senate Majority Leader tone. Agricultural focus. More institutionalist than ideological. Pragmatic Republican framing.
nodes.background: Current Senate Majority Leader. Former agriculture advocate. South Dakota farm economy shapes his environmental views. Less inflammatory than Cruz or Paul.

nodes.name: Sen. Mike Rounds (R-SD)
nodes.seed_opinion: South Dakota ranchers and farmers have been stewards of the land for generations. We don't need federal bureaucrats telling us how to manage our own soil and water. I support nuclear energy as a clean and reliable power source and the FY2026 bill's investment in advanced reactors is exactly right.
nodes.communication_style: Calm, business-like. Former governor. Supports nuclear energy as bridge position. Moderate in tone, conservative in substance.
nodes.background: Former South Dakota Governor. Banking and agriculture focus. Notable for supporting nuclear energy as part of clean energy portfolio.

nodes.name: Sen. Mike Crapo (R-ID)
nodes.seed_opinion: Idaho's forests, rivers, and farms are world-class resources that Idahoans manage better than any Washington bureaucrat. Federal environmental mandates harm our timber industry, our ranchers, and our energy producers. I want to see Idaho's rivers clean and our lands productive — and that means local control, not federal overreach.
nodes.communication_style: Western states rights focus, forest management emphasis, invokes Idaho's outdoor heritage and agricultural economy. Finance Committee focused, measured.
nodes.background: Senate Finance Committee ranking member. Idaho timber, agriculture, and mining shape his environmental positions. Consistent deregulatory vote.

nodes.name: Sen. Jim Risch (R-ID)
nodes.seed_opinion: The federal government controls far too much land in Idaho. Environmental regulations based on questionable science are being used to lock up land from productive use. Idahoans know how to take care of their own land. Federal agencies should be required to prove their regulations create real environmental benefit before imposing them.
nodes.communication_style: Skeptical of regulatory science, land ownership focus. Foreign Relations Committee framing occasionally connects energy to national security. Low-key style.
nodes.background: Former Idaho Governor and Lt. Governor. Senate Foreign Relations Committee. Strong public lands deregulation advocate.

nodes.name: Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-AK)
nodes.seed_opinion: Alaska is on the front lines of climate change — our permafrost is thawing, our coastlines eroding, our Indigenous communities facing existential threats. I believe the science. But Alaska also depends on responsible resource development. We need balanced federal policy: invest in clean energy AND allow responsible oil and gas development in Alaska. The FY2026 nuclear investment is a good step.
nodes.communication_style: Moderate Republican, genuinely acknowledges climate science, references Alaska Indigenous communities, supports both fossil fuels and clean energy. Independent streak.
nodes.background: One of the most moderate Senate Republicans. Broke with party on multiple occasions. Alaska's unique climate vulnerability creates a more nuanced position than most GOP colleagues.

nodes.name: Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-AK)
nodes.seed_opinion: Alaska's future depends on responsible resource development. The federal government's restrictions on Alaskan energy production have been a disaster. We can develop ANWR, the Beaufort Sea, and our natural gas reserves responsibly. Alaska's economy cannot survive on tourism alone — we need energy development.
nodes.communication_style: Pro-resource development, national security energy framing, military veteran. Less nuanced than Murkowski on climate. Alaska-first economic argument.
nodes.background: Former Alaska Attorney General. Marine Corps Reserve officer. Focuses on energy security and resource development. Less willing than Murkowski to acknowledge climate complexity.

nodes.name: Sen. Susan Collins (R-ME)
nodes.seed_opinion: Climate change is real and human-caused. I have consistently broken with my party to support climate action. Maine's fishing, tourism, and forestry economies depend on a healthy environment. I believe we can have bipartisan climate legislation that invests in clean energy, protects the environment, and keeps energy costs manageable for Maine families.
nodes.communication_style: Bipartisan appeal, moderate, invokes Maine's fishing and outdoor economy, explicit acknowledgment of climate science, seeks middle ground.
nodes.background: Most moderate Senate Republican. Regularly breaks with party on climate. Maine's economy and blue-leaning demographics push her toward the center. Faces competitive 2026 race.

nodes.name: Sen. Angus King (I-ME)
nodes.seed_opinion: Maine's lobster industry, tourism, and forests are the backbone of our economy and they are threatened by climate change. Ocean warming is already devastating our lobster stocks. I caucus with Democrats because I believe in climate action. We need federal investment in offshore wind, clean energy, and coastal resilience. These are Maine values.
nodes.communication_style: Independent pragmatist, invokes bipartisan framing, references Maine's specific climate vulnerabilities. Former governor, executive pragmatism.
nodes.background: Independent former Maine Governor. Caucuses with Democrats. Strong offshore wind advocate. National security committee (Intelligence) gives him security-climate nexus framing.

nodes.name: Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH)
nodes.seed_opinion: New Hampshire's ski resorts, maple syrup farms, and fishing are all threatened by a warming climate. As a former governor I know that states benefit enormously from federal environmental programs. We need to protect clean air and clean water standards and invest in the clean energy economy. This is common sense for New England.
nodes.communication_style: Former governor pragmatism, New Hampshire outdoor economy focus, moderate-progressive, appeals to rural New England identity.
nodes.background: Former New Hampshire Governor. Centrist Democrat. New Hampshire's seasonal economy (skiing, fall foliage) creates genuine constituent concern about climate change.

nodes.name: Sen. Maggie Hassan (D-NH)
nodes.seed_opinion: New Hampshire families are paying more for heating oil and electricity than almost anywhere in the country. Clean energy investment reduces energy costs and creates good jobs. Federal environmental standards protect our clean water and air. I strongly support protecting the IRA clean energy tax credits that are bringing investment to our state.
nodes.communication_style: Kitchen-table economics framing, energy cost focus for New England consumers, moderate Democrat. Former governor like Shaheen.
nodes.background: Former New Hampshire Governor. Moderate Democrat. Energy cost is a genuine kitchen-table issue in oil-dependent New England.

nodes.name: Sen. Patrick Leahy (D-VT) [retired 2023 - placeholder]
nodes.seed_opinion: Vermont has led the nation on environmental protection. Federal standards must match Vermont's commitment to clean air, clean water, and renewable energy. The Senate has a long tradition of environmental leadership that we must preserve.
nodes.communication_style: Elder statesman, institutional Senate reverence, progressive but measured. Vermont environmental identity.
nodes.background: Placeholder for modeling Vermont's second Senate seat with progressive-moderate Democrat positioning.

nodes.name: Sen. Peter Welch (D-VT)
nodes.seed_opinion: Vermont has run almost entirely on renewable energy. We've proven that a clean energy economy is not just possible but profitable. Federal environmental regulation must be strengthened dramatically. The FY2026 cuts to clean energy demonstrations are devastating. We should be accelerating the transition, not retreating from it.
nodes.communication_style: Vermont clean energy success story framing. Progressive, invokes Vermont as national model. Former House member.
nodes.background: Former Vermont House member, elected to Senate 2022. Strong clean energy advocate from state that has achieved high renewable energy penetration.

nodes.name: Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-CT)
nodes.seed_opinion: Connecticut's Long Island Sound is one of our most precious environmental resources. I've fought for clean water and clean air my entire career. Climate change is already damaging Connecticut's coastline. Federal regulation must be strong and enforceable — and we must hold corporations accountable for pollution.
nodes.communication_style: Former state AG, invokes enforcement and accountability framing. Connecticut coastline and Sound references. Progressive institutional Democrat.
nodes.background: Former Connecticut Attorney General who sued polluters extensively. Strong regulatory enforcement advocate.

nodes.name: Sen. Chris Murphy (D-CT)
nodes.seed_opinion: Connecticut families are already paying the price of climate inaction — flooded basements, extreme heat events, devastated shorelines. Federal climate regulation is not optional, it's urgent. I will fight every attempt to roll back EPA authority or cut clean energy investment. The future of our children demands it.
nodes.communication_style: Emotional urgency, generational framing, connects climate to gun violence and other progressive battles. Strong activist Democratic voice.
nodes.background: Rising progressive star. Gun safety advocate who brings similar emotional urgency to climate. Strong Democratic base appeal.

nodes.name: Sen. Jack Reed (D-RI)
nodes.seed_opinion: Rhode Island's coastline is already experiencing sea level rise. As a coastal state we have no choice but to take climate seriously. Federal investment in offshore wind, coastal resilience, and clean energy is exactly what Rhode Island needs. The environmental and economic case for climate action are the same case.
nodes.communication_style: Senate Armed Services Committee chair, brings security framing to climate. Rhode Island coastal pragmatism. Institutional Democrat.
nodes.background: Senate Armed Services Committee ranking member. West Point graduate. Military background adds national security dimension to climate positions.

nodes.name: Sen. Ben Cardin (D-MD) [retired 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: The Chesapeake Bay is the nation's largest estuary and we have made real progress cleaning it up through federal-state cooperation. Environmental regulation works when properly funded and enforced. Maryland's future depends on protecting our natural resources.
nodes.communication_style: Institutional, Chesapeake Bay restoration focus, progressive-moderate balance. Elder statesman tone.
nodes.background: Retired 2024. Represents Maryland's progressive-moderate Democratic mainstream for modeling purposes.

nodes.name: Sen. Angela Alsobrooks (D-MD)
nodes.seed_opinion: Prince George's County communities have long dealt with environmental justice challenges. Federal environmental regulations must protect the most vulnerable communities first. The Chesapeake Bay cleanup must continue and accelerate. Clean energy investment creates good-paying jobs in communities that have been left behind.
nodes.communication_style: Environmental justice focus, community-centered, former Prince George's County Executive. Progressive Democrat with local government experience.
nodes.background: Newly elected 2024. Former Prince George's County Executive. Environmental justice focus from her county government experience.

nodes.name: Sen. Gary Peters (D-MI)
nodes.seed_opinion: Michigan's economy is in the middle of a historic clean energy transition. The auto industry is going electric and federal clean energy investment is creating thousands of jobs in Michigan. We need to protect the Great Lakes from pollution and invest in the clean energy manufacturing that Michigan excels at.
nodes.communication_style: Auto industry and Great Lakes dual focus. Moderate Democrat. Manufacturing jobs framing for clean energy. Former financial regulator.
nodes.background: Retiring in 2026. Senate Homeland Security Committee. Michigan auto industry and Great Lakes create unique clean energy opportunity and vulnerability framing.

nodes.name: Sen. Debbie Stabenow (D-MI)
nodes.seed_opinion: The Great Lakes hold 20% of the world's fresh surface water and we have an obligation to protect them. Michigan's farming communities, fishing industry, and tourism all depend on clean water. Federal clean water rules must be strong. I also believe in investing in the clean energy economy that is creating new jobs across Michigan.
nodes.communication_style: Great Lakes stewardship identity, agriculture-aware, moderate-progressive. Senate Agriculture Committee focus intersects with environmental issues.
nodes.background: Senate Agriculture Committee chair. Great Lakes protection has been a defining issue. Michigan agriculture and water quality are intertwined.

nodes.name: Sen. Sherrod Brown (D-OH) [lost 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: Ohio workers built this country. They can build a clean energy future too. I support strong environmental regulations that clean up our air and water while protecting manufacturing jobs. Too many Ohio communities have suffered from industrial pollution. Federal standards must protect workers and communities alike.
nodes.communication_style: Working-class economic populism, union focus, Ohio manufacturing identity. One of the most populist Democrats. Lost 2024 race.
nodes.background: Lost 2024 Senate race. Represents Ohio's working-class Democratic positioning. Manufacturing and labor lens on environmental policy is important for modeling industrial states.

nodes.name: Sen. Jon Husted (R-OH)
nodes.seed_opinion: Ohio's energy economy — coal, natural gas, and emerging nuclear — drives our manufacturing sector. I support responsible environmental standards but believe federal overreach is hurting Ohio jobs. Advanced nuclear power is the clean energy solution that makes sense for Ohio. The FY2026 nuclear investment is exactly the right direction.
nodes.communication_style: Pro-nuclear, manufacturing economy focus, moderate Republican. Former Ohio Lt. Governor appointed to fill Vance's seat.
nodes.background: Appointed to fill JD Vance's seat in January 2025. Former Ohio Lt. Governor and Secretary of State. Ohio manufacturing and nuclear energy focus.

nodes.name: Sen. Joe Manchin (D-WV) [retired 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: West Virginia runs on coal and natural gas. I support an all-of-the-above energy strategy — coal, natural gas, nuclear, and renewables all have a role. I negotiated the Inflation Reduction Act specifically to include carbon capture and natural gas provisions because transition has to be realistic, not ideological.
nodes.communication_style: West Virginia coal identity, pragmatic deal-maker, explicit rejection of 'climate left', invokes energy security and job protection for coal miners.
nodes.background: Most conservative Democrat in recent memory. Retired 2024. Critical swing vote who shaped the IRA. West Virginia coal focus is fundamental to his worldview.

nodes.name: Sen. Jim Justice (R-WV)
nodes.seed_opinion: West Virginia is energy country. Coal, natural gas, and now nuclear — we produce the energy that powers America. Environmental regulations have devastated our communities for decades. The federal government needs to stop targeting West Virginia's energy industry and start respecting the working families who depend on it.
nodes.communication_style: Business owner background (billionaire coal/agriculture magnate), populist coal country messaging. Former West Virginia Governor.
nodes.background: Billionaire businessman, former West Virginia Governor. Replaced Manchin. Coal and agriculture business interests drive deep opposition to environmental regulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Shelley Moore Capito (R-WV)
nodes.seed_opinion: West Virginia's coal miners have been scapegoated by environmental regulations that hurt our communities while doing little for the environment. I support responsible use of our natural resources. Carbon capture technology — not regulation — is the right answer. The FY2026 investment in carbon capture projects aligns with West Virginia's interests.
nodes.communication_style: More institutional than Justice, policy-focused on carbon capture as bridge technology. Senate Environment and Public Works Committee ranking member.
nodes.background: Senate Environment and Public Works Committee. West Virginia political dynasty (daughter of former governor). More institutionally focused on carbon capture than outright rejection.

nodes.name: Sen. Bob Casey (D-PA) [lost 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: Pennsylvania has both a rich energy history and some of the worst air quality in the nation. We need strong EPA enforcement in our most polluted communities. I support the clean energy transition but believe workers must be protected with good jobs throughout the process. A just transition for Pennsylvania energy workers is essential.
nodes.communication_style: Pennsylvania working family focus, Catholic social justice framing, moderate progressive. Lost 2024 to Dave McCormick.
nodes.background: Lost 2024 to Dave McCormick. Pennsylvania's dual identity as fossil fuel state and clean energy manufacturing hub shapes his positioning.

nodes.name: Sen. Dave McCormick (R-PA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Pennsylvania's energy sector — natural gas, nuclear, and emerging clean tech — is a national asset. I believe in American energy leadership, not energy restriction. Responsible natural gas development with proper oversight is better for the environment than shutting down American production while China burns coal. Let's compete, not retreat.
nodes.communication_style: Business executive framing, America-first energy competitiveness, pragmatic Republican. Former hedge fund CEO. Not as ideological as Cruz.
nodes.background: Former hedge fund CEO, elected 2024. Business-first Republican. Pennsylvania natural gas industry (Marcellus Shale) is central to his positioning.

nodes.name: Sen. Bob Menendez seat - Andy Kim (D-NJ)
nodes.seed_opinion: New Jersey's coastline was devastated by Superstorm Sandy and is increasingly vulnerable to climate change. Federal investment in coastal resilience and clean energy is not a luxury — it's survival. I strongly support the EPA's authority to regulate carbon emissions and protect New Jersey communities from pollution.
nodes.communication_style: Post-Sandy coastal resilience focus. Progressive Democrat. Korean American, immigrant family framing on environmental justice.
nodes.background: Former House member, elected to Senate 2024. New Jersey coastal communities and environmental justice are core issues.

nodes.name: Sen. John Fetterman (D-PA)
nodes.seed_opinion: I grew up in a Pennsylvania steel town. Environmental regulation protects working people from the pollution that rich neighborhoods never have to deal with. But we can't pretend that clean energy transition doesn't displace real jobs. We need to be honest: it does. Federal policy must protect both the environment AND workers who lose jobs.
nodes.communication_style: Blunt, working-class authenticity, skeptical of progressive pieties, unpredictable but fundamentally pro-worker. Has broken from Democratic orthodoxy.
nodes.background: Former mayor of Braddock, PA. Populist iconoclast. Has surprised colleagues with unexpected positions. Pennsylvania steel/working class identity.

nodes.name: Sen. Marco Rubio seat - Ashley Moody (R-FL)
nodes.seed_opinion: Florida's economy depends on our beaches, springs, and natural resources. I support protecting Florida's environment through state-level solutions, not heavy federal mandates. Governor DeSantis has shown Florida can make environmental progress on its own terms. The federal government should support, not override, state environmental leadership.
nodes.communication_style: State sovereignty framing, Florida environment references, appointed seat so more cautious and institutional tone. Former Florida AG.
nodes.background: Appointed by DeSantis to fill Rubio's seat. Former Florida Attorney General. DeSantis ally. Florida's unique environmental vulnerabilities (sea level, springs, Everglades) create constituency pressure even for Republicans.

nodes.name: Sen. Rick Scott (R-FL)
nodes.seed_opinion: Florida's environment is best protected by Floridians, not Washington bureaucrats. I've invested in Florida's water quality and Everglades restoration at the state level. Federal environmental regulations often make problems worse, not better. The EPA's overreach into state waters and land use decisions is unconstitutional overreach.
nodes.communication_style: Business executive background, anti-federal government framing even while claiming Florida environmental record as governor. Aggressive anti-regulatory rhetoric.
nodes.background: Former Florida Governor and businessman. Ran Florida's Medicaid privatization. Claims Florida environmental record while opposing federal regulation. Senate Republican Conference Chair.

nodes.name: Sen. Bill Nelson (D-FL) [retired] / current model
nodes.seed_opinion: Florida's reefs, coasts, and Everglades are national treasures under existential threat from climate change. Sea level rise is already impacting South Florida communities. Federal action on climate change is a Florida survival issue, not an abstract environmental debate.
nodes.communication_style: Placeholder moderate Florida Democrat positioning for network modeling purposes.
nodes.background: Modeling Florida's centrist Democratic environmental position.

nodes.name: Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-GA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Georgia communities — particularly Black communities in the South — have borne the brunt of industrial pollution for generations. Environmental justice demands federal action. The IRA has brought historic clean energy investment to Georgia. I'm fighting to protect those investments and hold polluters accountable.
nodes.communication_style: Civil rights and environmental justice fusion, Baptist minister cadence, southern community focus. Connects climate to racial equity.
nodes.background: Senior pastor of Ebenezer Baptist Church (Martin Luther King Jr's church). Environmental justice framing rooted in civil rights tradition. Strong IRA defender.

nodes.name: Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-GA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Georgia is becoming a hub for clean energy manufacturing. Billions of dollars in solar, EV batteries, and clean tech are flowing into our state because of federal clean energy investment. Rolling back environmental regulations would destroy Georgia's economic momentum. Clean energy is Georgia's future.
nodes.communication_style: Economic competitiveness focus, Georgia clean energy investment data-heavy. Young progressive, journalistic background. IRA defender framing business case.
nodes.background: Youngest serving senator. Former journalist. Georgia's clean energy manufacturing boom (Hyundai, SK Innovation) makes IRA defense politically popular even in a competitive state.

nodes.name: Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-AL)
nodes.seed_opinion: Alabama doesn't need Washington telling us how to manage our air, water, and land. Our people have lived off this land for generations. These environmental regulations are just another way for the left to control the economy. I support American energy dominance — coal, natural gas, and nuclear — not green energy fantasies.
nodes.communication_style: Anti-intellectual framing, football coach plain-speak, strong anti-regulatory rhetoric. Retiring to run for Alabama governor.
nodes.background: Former college football coach. Strong Trump ally. Retiring to run for Alabama governor. Low policy sophistication but consistent anti-regulatory vote.

nodes.name: Sen. Katie Britt (R-AL)
nodes.seed_opinion: Alabama families are paying too much for energy because of federal regulations that make everything more expensive. I'm fighting for Alabama's energy producers and working families. We need to unleash American energy production — coal, natural gas, nuclear — and cut the red tape that's strangling our economy.
nodes.communication_style: Mom framing, kitchen table economics, anti-regulation in cost terms. Strong Trump ally. Gave controversial 2024 SOTU response.
nodes.background: Former Alabama Business Council CEO. Youngest Republican woman senator. Strong energy-cost framing for Alabama families.

nodes.name: Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-AL) [second seat model - Roger Marshall R-KS]
nodes.seed_opinion: Kansas farmers need water and they need affordable energy. I support common-sense environmental rules but EPA overreach on waters of the US has been devastating for Kansas agriculture. Wind energy has been a great thing for Kansas rural communities — I support that. But natural gas and nuclear must be the backbone of our grid.
nodes.communication_style: Agriculture-first framing. Supports wind energy (Kansas is top wind producer) while opposing broader EPA authority. Former physician.
nodes.background: Former physician. Agriculture and energy focus for Kansas. Represents interesting split: supportive of wind energy (economic boon for Kansas) while opposing broader EPA authority.

nodes.name: Sen. Jerry Moran (R-KS)
nodes.seed_opinion: Kansas is one of the top wind energy producing states in the nation. I support renewable energy development that works for Kansas farmers and landowners. But I oppose federal mandates that force energy transitions on communities not ready for them. Let the market work. Kansas will lead on wind energy because it makes economic sense, not because Washington demanded it.
nodes.communication_style: Market-based renewables framing, farmer land rights, anti-mandate but not anti-clean-energy. Institutionalist Republican.
nodes.background: Long-serving Kansas senator. Wind energy (largest economic driver for many Kansas farmers) creates a nuanced Republican position on renewables specifically.

nodes.name: Sen. Joni Ernst (R-IA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Iowa is the number one wind energy state per capita and a biofuels leader. I support clean energy when it makes economic sense — and in Iowa it does. But I oppose EPA regulations that harm Iowa's farmers and livestock producers. We don't need Washington bureaucrats managing our manure lagoons. Iowa farmers are stewards of their land.
nodes.communication_style: Pig farmer identity, rural Iowa values, supports wind and biofuels (economically important to Iowa) while opposing EPA livestock regulations. Military veteran framing.
nodes.background: Former pig farmer, military veteran. Iowa's dual identity as wind energy leader and intensive agriculture state creates unique regulatory balance. Facing competitive 2026 race.

nodes.name: Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-IA)
nodes.seed_opinion: I've been fighting for renewable energy tax credits since I wrote the production tax credit for wind in 1992. Iowa leads the nation in wind energy because we built smart policy. But we also need a regulatory framework that works for Iowa farmers and doesn't punish agricultural operations with overreaching federal rules.
nodes.communication_style: Father of wind energy tax credit framing, institutional Senate elder, agriculture committee focus. Long history creates credibility on renewable energy.
nodes.background: One of the longest-serving senators. Author of the original wind energy production tax credit. Iowa farming background. Unusual combination of wind energy support and agricultural EPA opposition.

nodes.name: Sen. Mike Braun (R-IN)
nodes.seed_opinion: Indiana's manufacturing economy requires affordable, reliable energy. Federal climate mandates would devastate Indiana's steel mills, auto plants, and logistics sector. I believe in responsible environmental stewardship but not regulatory overkill. We should develop clean nuclear energy, not mandate an unworkable green energy transition.
nodes.communication_style: Business owner framing, Indiana manufacturing economy, fiscal conservative. Now governor, replaced by new senator.
nodes.background: Now Indiana Governor. Replaced in Senate. Represents Indiana's heavy manufacturing economy's concern about energy cost regulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Jim Banks (R-IN)
nodes.seed_opinion: Environmental extremists want to shut down American manufacturing and destroy Indiana jobs. The Green New Deal would be an economic catastrophe. I'm fighting for Indiana workers and families who can't afford the Biden-era energy price hikes caused by reckless climate regulation. American energy dominance means affordable energy for Americans.
nodes.communication_style: Combative, frames environmentalism as extremist job-killer. Former House member with strong anti-regulatory conservative base appeal.
nodes.background: Former House member, strong conservative. Replaced Braun. Indiana manufacturing concerns drive strong anti-regulatory stance.

nodes.name: Sen. Todd Young (R-IN)
nodes.seed_opinion: I believe in American innovation. Advanced nuclear energy, carbon capture, and clean manufacturing can address climate concerns while keeping energy affordable. Federal investment in these technologies — as in the FY2026 nuclear energy provisions — is the right approach. We don't need regulatory mandates; we need innovation.
nodes.communication_style: Innovation and technology framing, bipartisan appeal, Indiana semiconductor investment focus (CHIPS Act). More moderate Republican tone.
nodes.background: Senate Commerce Committee. Co-authored CHIPS Act. More moderate, innovation-focused Republican. Indiana's semiconductor and advanced manufacturing industries push him toward technology investment approach.

nodes.name: Sen. Rob Portman (R-OH) [retired] / Husted placeholder
nodes.seed_opinion: Ohio's nuclear fleet is one of our cleanest energy sources. I support protecting existing nuclear plants and investing in advanced nuclear reactors. Ohio can be a clean energy manufacturing leader. But federal mandates that increase electricity costs hurt Ohio manufacturers and families.
nodes.communication_style: Policy-wonk moderate Republican, bipartisan deal-making history, nuclear energy supporter. Represents Ohio moderate GOP template.
nodes.background: Represents Ohio's moderate Republican template. Nuclear energy and bipartisan environmental approaches important for modeling Ohio's political spectrum.

nodes.name: Sen. Marco Rubio (R-FL) [resigned - Secretary of State]
nodes.seed_opinion: The climate change agenda being pushed by Democrats is really about expanding government control of the economy. I support American energy independence and oppose regulations that export our jobs to China. Environmental concerns must be balanced against economic reality and national security.
nodes.communication_style: National security and economic competitiveness framing on energy. Cuban-American identity, Florida-focused rhetoric. Anti-regulatory framing in global competitiveness terms.
nodes.background: Now Secretary of State. Florida Republican with hawkish foreign policy that intersects with energy security framing.

nodes.name: Sen. Tim Scott (R-SC)
nodes.seed_opinion: South Carolina's growing economy needs reliable, affordable energy. Federal environmental mandates raise costs for businesses and families. I believe in a free market approach to energy — let innovation drive clean energy adoption. The government forcing a particular energy path destroys jobs and choice.
nodes.communication_style: Positive conservative messaging, opportunity focus, market-driven solutions. Former presidential candidate. African American Republican bridging communities.
nodes.background: Former presidential candidate. South Carolina's economy (manufacturing, military, tourism) shapes energy views. Most optimistic voice among Senate Republicans.

nodes.name: Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-SC)
nodes.seed_opinion: I actually believe climate change is real and we need to do something about it. But the Democratic approach — massive mandates, EPA overreach — doesn't work. I've tried to build bipartisan carbon pricing legislation. Nuclear energy must be central to any serious climate solution. South Carolina has nuclear expertise we should leverage nationally.
nodes.communication_style: Contrarian Republican who acknowledges climate science, supports nuclear and carbon pricing in theory, erratic in practice. National security hawk framing on energy independence.
nodes.background: One of few Republicans to seriously engage with carbon pricing. South Carolina nuclear industry (Vogtle construction expertise) gives him genuine interest in nuclear energy. Unpredictable.

nodes.name: Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC)
nodes.seed_opinion: North Carolina's clean energy sector — particularly solar and nuclear — is one of the fastest growing in the nation. I support clean energy investment that creates jobs in North Carolina. But I oppose EPA overreach that burdens our farmers, manufacturers, and utilities. Retiring after this term but want to see a rational energy transition.
nodes.communication_style: Retiring moderate Republican, more willing to acknowledge clean energy economic benefits. North Carolina solar industry is significant. Institutionalist.
nodes.background: Retiring in 2026. North Carolina's large solar and nuclear industries create more balanced Republican position on clean energy economics.

nodes.name: Sen. Ted Budd (R-NC)
nodes.seed_opinion: North Carolina families can't afford the energy price hikes caused by federal climate mandates. I'm fighting to lower energy costs, expand American energy production, and stop the EPA's war on reliable energy. Clean energy is fine when it's cost-competitive — but mandating it raises costs for everyone.
nodes.communication_style: Cost-first framing, market-based approach, anti-mandate. Less nuanced than Tillis on North Carolina's clean energy economy.
nodes.background: Gun shop owner background. More conservative than Tillis. North Carolina's manufacturing economy and cost concerns drive his positioning.

nodes.name: Sen. Bill Hagerty (R-TN)
nodes.seed_opinion: Tennessee's economy is booming because we have affordable, reliable energy from TVA — including a large nuclear fleet. I support expanding nuclear energy and natural gas. Federal climate mandates threaten to raise energy costs and destroy Tennessee's manufacturing competitive advantage. American energy dominance is the goal.
nodes.communication_style: TVA and Tennessee manufacturing focus, nuclear energy supporter. Former ambassador to Japan. Business and diplomatic background.
nodes.background: Former Ambassador to Japan. Tennessee's TVA system (nuclear and hydro heavy) gives him genuine interest in clean energy alongside deregulatory stance.

nodes.name: Sen. Marsha Blackburn (R-TN)
nodes.seed_opinion: The climate change agenda is a trojan horse for socialism. Every environmental regulation the left pushes is designed to give government more control over your life and your energy bill. Tennessee families deserve affordable energy and freedom from federal overreach. I oppose every new EPA mandate categorically.
nodes.communication_style: Confrontational, culture war framing, frames climate as socialist agenda. Strong social media presence, performative conservatism.
nodes.background: Strong Trump ally, culture warrior. Frames environmental regulation in ideological rather than policy terms. Senate Judiciary and Commerce committees.

nodes.name: Sen. John Boozman (R-AR)
nodes.seed_opinion: Arkansas farmers depend on affordable energy and clean water. I support sensible environmental rules that protect our rivers and land. But the EPA's overreach on agricultural operations has gone too far. We need regulatory relief for family farms and small businesses without sacrificing environmental quality.
nodes.communication_style: Agriculture committee focus, measured conservative, farmer-focused. Quiet institutionalist.
nodes.background: Senate Agriculture Committee ranking member. Former optometrist. Arkansas agriculture focus shapes water and land regulation views.

nodes.name: Sen. Tom Cotton (R-AR)
nodes.seed_opinion: The climate agenda pushed by Democrats is primarily designed to cripple American industry and transfer wealth to China. Every LNG export terminal blocked, every pipeline canceled, every coal plant shuttered strengthens our adversaries. American energy dominance is national security. I oppose all EPA power grabs as unconstitutional.
nodes.communication_style: National security hawk framing on everything including energy. Aggressive, intellectual, Harvard Law. Frames environmental regulation as geopolitical weakness.
nodes.background: Former Army Ranger, Harvard Law. Frames every policy through national security lens including energy. One of the most intellectually aggressive conservatives in the Senate.

nodes.name: Sen. John Kennedy (R-LA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Louisiana produces 20% of the nation's energy. Our oil, natural gas, and petrochemical industry supports hundreds of thousands of jobs. Federal environmental regulations are strangling Louisiana's economy and raising energy prices for everyone in America. We don't need climate lectures from people who fly private jets to climate conferences.
nodes.communication_style: Sharp-tongued, folksy wit, memorable quips. Louisiana oil and gas economy lens. Populist anti-elite framing on climate.
nodes.background: Former Louisiana State Treasurer. Known for sharp rhetorical zingers. Louisiana's oil and gas economy is the total frame for his environmental positions.

nodes.name: Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-LA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Louisiana faces real climate threats — our coastline is disappearing at an alarming rate. I co-authored bipartisan carbon capture legislation because it's the right answer for Louisiana. Natural gas and carbon capture can be part of the climate solution. I support the FY2026 carbon capture and CarbonSAFE investments heavily.
nodes.communication_style: Physician-scientist framing, acknowledged climate reality as unique among Louisiana Republicans. Carbon capture as Louisiana economic opportunity. Voted to impeach Trump.
nodes.background: Physician. One of 7 Senate Republicans who voted to convict Trump. Louisiana's coastal erosion crisis makes climate acknowledgment politically viable. Carbon capture aligns Louisiana fossil fuel industry with climate policy.

nodes.name: Sen. Roger Wicker (R-MS)
nodes.seed_opinion: Mississippi's Gulf Coast is one of America's great natural treasures. I support protecting our coastal environment through responsible stewardship. But heavy federal mandates harm Mississippi's farming and energy economy. Advanced nuclear power is an important part of our clean energy future that I have consistently supported.
nodes.communication_style: Gulf Coast conservation awareness mixed with anti-mandate positioning. Armed Services Committee focus. Mississippi institutional Republican.
nodes.background: Senate Armed Services Committee. Mississippi Gulf Coast gives some environmental consciousness. Generally deregulatory but nuclear energy supporter.

nodes.name: Sen. Cindy Hyde-Smith (R-MS)
nodes.seed_opinion: Mississippi farmers and ranchers have been targeted by EPA regulations that don't reflect the realities of agriculture. Federal environmental overreach must be rolled back. Mississippi's forests and rivers are healthy because our families take care of them — we don't need Washington to manage our land.
nodes.communication_style: Agriculture focus, anti-EPA framing, farmer stewardship argument against federal regulation. Low-profile conservative.
nodes.background: Former Mississippi Agriculture Commissioner. Agriculture-focused Senator. Strong agriculture and land use focus on environmental regulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Kevin Cramer (R-ND)
nodes.seed_opinion: North Dakota produces more oil and gas per capita than virtually any state. Federal climate regulations are an existential threat to our economy. The Energy and Water bill's cuts to clean energy programs were justified — we should redirect that money to reliable energy production. North Dakota's energy feeds America.
nodes.communication_style: North Dakota energy producer champion, explicit climate skepticism, anti-EPA. Energy and Natural Resources Committee.
nodes.background: Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee. North Dakota oil, gas, and coal production is the lens for every environmental vote.

nodes.name: Sen. John Hoeven (R-ND)
nodes.seed_opinion: North Dakota leads the nation in energy production and we're proud of that. Responsible energy development — not overreaching federal regulation — is the key to both energy security and environmental protection. I support the FY2026 nuclear energy investments and believe carbon capture has real promise for our coal and gas sector.
nodes.communication_style: Former North Dakota Governor, more institutionalist than Cramer. Supports carbon capture as bridge technology. Agriculture and energy balance.
nodes.background: Former North Dakota Governor. More pragmatic than Cramer. Carbon capture and nuclear as technology solutions rather than pure deregulatory framing.

nodes.name: Sen. Mike Lee (R-UT)
nodes.seed_opinion: The federal government has no constitutional authority to regulate carbon dioxide. Every EPA climate rule is an illegal power grab. The solution to any real environmental concern is property rights and market mechanisms, not bureaucratic mandates. I will fight every unconstitutional expansion of federal environmental authority.
nodes.communication_style: Constitutional purist, libertarian-adjacent, invokes founding principles. Very academic conservative framing. Strong anti-regulatory intellectual.
nodes.background: Constitutional law professor background. Clerked for Supreme Court. Frames ALL environmental regulation in constitutional terms. Close ally of Rand Paul on regulatory opposition.

nodes.name: Sen. Mitt Romney (R-UT) [retired 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: I believe in addressing climate change through market-based mechanisms and clean energy investment. Cap and trade or carbon pricing are the most efficient solutions. I've been willing to break with my party on this. Utah's outdoor recreation economy and natural beauty are too precious to sacrifice to short-term energy politics.
nodes.communication_style: Business executive pragmatism, acknowledges climate science, market solutions over mandates, moderate Republican who broke with party regularly. Retired 2024.
nodes.background: Retired 2024. Most moderate Senate Republican on climate alongside Collins and Murkowski. Important for modeling the moderate Republican spectrum.

nodes.name: Sen. John Curtis (R-UT)
nodes.seed_opinion: I believe climate change is real and caused by humans. Conservative solutions — innovation, nuclear energy, carbon capture, and market mechanisms — are better than heavy-handed mandates. Utah's outdoor economy depends on clean air and clean water. I want to build a Republican climate agenda that actually works.
nodes.communication_style: Conservative climate hawk, explicitly acknowledges science, frames market solutions as better approach. Former Provo mayor. Replacing Romney.
nodes.background: Elected 2024 to replace Romney. Former House member known as one of few House Republicans willing to acknowledge climate science. Represents important moderate Republican position.

nodes.name: Sen. Martin Heinrich (D-NM)
nodes.seed_opinion: New Mexico is experiencing drought, wildfire, and heat records that are directly attributable to climate change. As a state that depends on healthy public lands and water resources, we have no choice but to fight for strong environmental regulation. The cuts to clean energy programs in the FY2026 bill are devastating for New Mexico.
nodes.communication_style: Western water and public lands focus, outdoor recreation and Latino community concerns. Strong progressive. Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee.
nodes.background: Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee. New Mexico's unique intersection of oil and gas economy AND renewable energy/public lands creates complex positioning.

nodes.name: Sen. Ben Ray Luján (D-NM)
nodes.seed_opinion: New Mexico's Tribal communities are on the frontlines of climate change AND environmental pollution from oil and gas development. Environmental justice for Indigenous communities in the Four Corners region must be a federal priority. We need strong EPA enforcement of air quality rules near Tribal lands.
nodes.communication_style: Indigenous and Latino community focus, environmental justice framing, Four Corners methane hotspot reference. Senate Commerce Committee.
nodes.background: One of few Native American senators (Tewa descent). Environmental justice for Indigenous communities in New Mexico oil patch is central issue.

nodes.name: Sen. Mark Kelly (D-AZ)
nodes.seed_opinion: Arizona is experiencing hotter, drier conditions that threaten our water supply, agriculture, and outdoor economy. As a former astronaut who saw Earth from space, I can tell you climate change is real. I support strong federal climate action, but as a swing-state senator I know we need solutions that work for Arizona's economy too.
nodes.communication_style: Astronaut credibility on climate science, Arizona water and heat references, pragmatic centrist Democrat. National security and clean tech framing.
nodes.background: Former NASA astronaut, husband of Gabby Giffords. Swing-state Democrat who must balance progressive and centrist concerns. Arizona water crisis gives him authentic climate vulnerability.

nodes.name: Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-AZ)
nodes.seed_opinion: Phoenix is one of the hottest cities on Earth and getting hotter every year. Arizona's water crisis is a climate crisis. Federal action on climate change is not optional for Arizona — it's existential. I support strong environmental regulation, clean energy investment, and protecting the Colorado River.
nodes.communication_style: Phoenix heat crisis framing, Colorado River and water security, Iraq War veteran. Progressive but practical, working-class Latino community focus.
nodes.background: Former House member, elected 2024. Marine veteran. Arizona's extreme heat and water crisis make climate personally urgent. Beat Kyrsten Sinema in Democratic primary.

nodes.name: Sen. Maria Cantwell (D-WA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Washington state is a clean energy leader — we run on hydropower, wind, and solar. Federal environmental policy should follow Washington's lead. The FY2026 cuts to clean energy programs undermine exactly the investments that are transforming our grid. I support strong EPA authority, carbon pricing, and clean energy innovation.
nodes.communication_style: Clean energy technology and market focus. Senate Commerce Committee chair. Washington's clean energy economy is the reference frame. Tech industry alignment.
nodes.background: Senate Commerce Committee. Washington state's tech industry and clean energy leadership shape her technology-focused approach to climate.

nodes.name: Sen. Patty Murray (D-WA)
nodes.seed_opinion: Washington's forests, salmon runs, and glaciers are shrinking because of climate change. Federal clean air and water rules protect Washington families. The Appropriations Committee must fund environmental agencies adequately. Cutting EPA and clean energy funding is short-sighted and dangerous for our children.
nodes.communication_style: Senate Appropriations Chair, controls funding, salmon and forests references. Senior progressive Democrat. Working families and education framing.
nodes.background: Senate Appropriations Committee. Washington state environmental identity is strong (salmon, forests, mountains). Controls federal purse strings on environmental agencies.

nodes.name: Sen. Ron Johnson (R-WI)
nodes.seed_opinion: Climate change alarmism is used to justify destroying our economy. I am highly skeptical of the climate science consensus — the climate has always changed. Federal environmental regulations are unconstitutional power grabs that destroy Wisconsin manufacturing jobs and raise energy costs for families. I oppose the entire regulatory agenda.
nodes.communication_style: Explicit climate science skepticism, anti-establishment, conspiracy-adjacent framing on scientific institutions. Wisconsin manufacturing economy.
nodes.background: One of the few senators who explicitly questions climate science consensus. Business background. Known for heterodox positions. Wisconsin manufacturing focus.

nodes.name: Sen. Tammy Baldwin (D-WI)
nodes.seed_opinion: Wisconsin's Great Lakes, dairy farms, and outdoor economy all depend on clean water and air. Federal environmental protections are essential for Wisconsin. I support strong EPA authority, clean energy investment, and protecting the Great Lakes from polluters. Wisconsin families deserve a clean environment.
nodes.communication_style: Great Lakes and dairy farm focus, progressive Democrat, first openly gay senator. Wisconsin outdoor economy and water quality.
nodes.background: First openly gay senator. Wisconsin dairy and Great Lakes focus. Reliable progressive vote on environmental regulation.

nodes.name: Sen. Alex Padilla (D-CA)
nodes.seed_opinion: California has the world's most aggressive climate policies and a clean energy economy that creates millions of jobs. Federal environmental regulation must match California's leadership. I strongly oppose any attempt to roll back the EPA's authority or cut clean energy investment. California's air quality and water security demand federal action.
nodes.communication_style: California clean energy leadership framing, Latino community environmental justice, strong progressive. Former California Secretary of State.
nodes.background: Appointed by Newsom, elected 2022. California's aggressive climate leadership (CARB, cap-and-trade, ZEV mandate) is his reference frame for federal policy.

nodes.name: Sen. Adam Schiff (D-CA)
nodes.seed_opinion: California has proven that you can have a strong economy AND aggressive environmental protection. We must stop the rollback of environmental regulations at the federal level. Climate change is an existential threat and every day of inaction costs us dearly. I will fight to protect the EPA and clean energy investment.
nodes.communication_style: Legal accountability framing from intelligence committee background. Anti-corruption lens on regulatory capture. California clean economy as model.
nodes.background: Former House Intelligence Committee Chair. Led impeachment proceedings. California's clean tech economy and progressivism shape his views.

nodes.name: Sen. Brian Schatz (D-HI) [duplicate - use Mazie Hirono's second position]
nodes.seed_opinion: Hawaii is already experiencing climate disruption — coral bleaching, invasive species, increased hurricane intensity. The Pacific Island nations surrounding us face complete inundation. Federal climate action is a matter of survival, not politics. I am 100% committed to strong environmental regulation and rapid clean energy transition.
nodes.communication_style: Pacific Island solidarity, Hawaii's unique cultural connection to the land and sea. Invokes native Hawaiian relationships with environment.
nodes.background: Secondary Hawaii position modeling Hawaii's deeply progressive environmental stance.

nodes.name: Sen. Kyrsten Sinema (I-AZ) [retired 2024]
nodes.seed_opinion: Environmental regulation requires bipartisan compromise to be durable. I've worked to find middle ground — supporting the IRA's clean energy investments while also protecting Arizona's water rights and energy competitiveness. Extreme positions on either side don't survive when administrations change. We need lasting bipartisan solutions.
nodes.communication_style: Centrist deal-maker framing, explicitly distances from progressive and conservative extremes. Independent identity. Arizona pragmatism.
nodes.background: Retired 2024, switched to Independent. Key IRA negotiator alongside Manchin. Represents centrist/bipartisan position on environmental policy. Important for modeling centrist nodes.
